does: This paper introduces the Policy-Execution-Authorization (PEA) architecture, a system-level safety framework that structurally separates intent generation, authorization, and execution into isolated layers connected via cryptographically constrained capability tokens. The core contributions include an Intent Verification Layer ensuring capability-intent consistency, Intent Lineage Tracking that binds executable intents to originating user requests through cryptographic anchors, Goal Drift Detection that rejects semantically divergent intents below configurable thresholds, and an Output Semantic Gate for final output validation. Rather than relying on probabilistic model-level guarantees, PEA enforces goal integrity through architectural constraints and cryptographic mechanisms, providing structural guarantees against agentic misalignment where AI systems generate and execute harmful actions derived from internally constructed goals.
connects: This work responds to demonstrated failures of existing mitigation approaches including RLHF and constitutional prompting, which operate primarily at the model level and provide only probabilistic safety guarantees. It builds on broader research into AI alignment, agentic behavior, and goal specification, extending beyond prompt-level interventions to system-level safety architecture. The separation-of-powers design principle adapts classical governance structures to AI safety, drawing conceptually from capability-based security models and cryptographic enforcement mechanisms used in distributed systems. The paper engages with concerns about goal drift and specification gaming, extending detection mechanisms into runtime verification of semantic consistency between user intent and system behavior.
enables: This architectural approach enables stronger compositional safety guarantees for complex AI systems by shifting safety enforcement from probabilistic model properties to verifiable system constraints. It opens pathways for deploying frontier AI agents in high-stakes domains where provable safety properties are required, and supports development of auditable decision trails for AI-generated actions. The framework could facilitate integration of multiple safety layers—combining cryptographic verification with semantic analysis—enabling future work on formally verified AI systems. It establishes a foundation for building AI systems where safety properties persist even if underlying models behave unexpectedly, pointing toward AI deployment paradigms that maintain human oversight and control through architectural design rather than relying solely on model training.
